label: Vietnam WTO accession shock — export surge then structural trap
sources: World Bank Vietnam Development Report 2008
IMF Article IV Vietnam 2012
OECD Vietnam Economic Survey 2009
context: Vietnam joined the WTO in January 2007 after 11 years of negotiations. The country was a lower-middle-income export economy heavily dependent on garments, footwear, and raw commodities. FDI surged post-accession but concentrated in assembly industries with thin domestic value chains.
shock_or_trigger: WTO accession triggered a 40% FDI surge in year 1, but also immediate import competition in agriculture and light manufacturing. Trade deficit widened sharply as imports rose faster than export capability. Inflation hit 23% by 2008, driven partly by commodity imports and credit expansion.
policy_response: Government doubled down on FDI attraction via industrial zones rather than SME development. State-owned enterprises (SOEs) were protected and expanded into real estate and finance. Infrastructure investment accelerated. Monetary policy was loose, enabling credit boom.

outcomes.export_diversification: Minimal — electronics (Samsung) became dominant by 2012 replacing garments but still concentrated
outcomes.institutional_development: Weak — SOE reform stalled, corruption increased with FDI inflows
outcomes.poverty_reduction: Continued but slowed relative to pre-WTO trend

causal_mechanisms: FDI-led growth without domestic linkages creates an enclave economy
Trade liberalisation without complementary SME support hollows out domestic manufacturing
Infrastructure prioritisation over institution-building delays second-stage development
Single large investor concentration (Samsung in Vietnam) creates fragility
lessons: Export surge from FDI attraction masks structural dependence. Without domestic capability building and value chain integration, the growth is borrowed — the rents flow out and the economy remains price-competitive only. The 2014-2022 electronics boom disguised that Vietnam's domestic sector never caught up.

label: Mauritius Export Processing Zone — managed diversification success
sources: Rodrik 2001 — The Global Governance of Trade as if Development Really Mattered
IMF Mauritius Article IV 1996
World Bank Mauritius Economic Memorandum 1992
context: Mauritius in 1970 was a sugar monoculture island economy with high unemployment and no manufacturing base. The government created Export Processing Zones with tax holidays, cheap labour, and trade preference access to Europe under the Lomé Convention. The strategy deliberately sequenced: EPZ first, services diversification second, financial centre third.
shock_or_trigger: Lomé Convention preferences gave Mauritius tariff-free access to the EC for garment exports. This was the initial anchor demand that made the EPZ viable. Without preference margins, the wage cost advantage alone would not have been sufficient.
policy_response: Disciplined industrial policy: EPZ operated in parallel with protected domestic sector. Government maintained fiscal surpluses through the boom. Education investment was front-loaded. When EU preferences eroded in the 1990s, the economy pivoted to tourism and financial services — capability built during EPZ phase was redeployed.

outcomes.gdp_growth: GDP per capita rose from $260 in 1970 to $3,500 in 1995 — 13x in 25 years
outcomes.export_diversification: Moved from 90% sugar to garments (40%), tourism (30%), sugar (20%), services (10%) by 1995
outcomes.fiscal_position: Fiscal surplus maintained throughout most of EPZ period
outcomes.institutional_development: Strong — independent judiciary, democratic elections, rule of law maintained
outcomes.poverty_reduction: Near elimination of absolute poverty by 1990

causal_mechanisms: Trade preferences provide a temporary comparative advantage window — must be used to build permanent capability
Sequenced diversification (goods → services → finance) worked because each phase built on prior capabilities
Fiscal discipline during boom creates buffer for structural transition
Political stability under competitive democracy enabled long-horizon planning
lessons: Trade preference windows are time-limited. Mauritius succeeded because it used the preference window to build education, infrastructure, and institutions simultaneously — not just to maximise short-term export revenue. The EPZ itself was a vehicle for capability accumulation, not an end state.

label: Bangladesh garment export lock-in — growth without structural transformation
sources: World Bank Bangladesh Development Update 2019
ILO Better Work Bangladesh Programme Evaluation 2015
CPD Bangladesh Economy Report 2020
context: Bangladesh became the world's second largest garment exporter after China by 2012. The sector employed 4 million workers (80% women) and accounted for 84% of total exports. GDP growth averaged 6-7% consistently. But the economy remained structurally dependent on one sector, one buyer network (EU/US), and one competitive advantage (cheap labour).
shock_or_trigger: The 2013 Rana Plaza factory collapse (1,134 dead) triggered Western buyer scrutiny and compliance costs that favoured larger factories, accelerating consolidation. The deeper structural issue was already apparent: despite 20 years of garment growth, Bangladesh had not developed a second export sector at scale.
policy_response: Government response was compliance-heavy: factory safety reforms, wage increases, Bangladesh Accord. But no equivalent effort on diversification. Pharmaceutical, leather, and IT sectors received policy attention but not the same concentration of fiscal incentives. RMG continued to absorb most export infrastructure investment.

outcomes.gdp_growth: Maintained 6-8% through period despite Rana Plaza
outcomes.export_diversification: RMG share of exports remained 83-85% — no meaningful diversification
outcomes.fiscal_position: Stable, remittances provided a second pillar
outcomes.institutional_development: Weak — labour rights remained restricted, political competition constrained
outcomes.poverty_reduction: Significant — poverty rate fell from 40% (2005) to 20% (2020)

causal_mechanisms: Export concentration creates political economy lock-in — the dominant sector captures trade and labour policy
Success in one sector crowds out policy bandwidth for diversification
Low-wage competitive advantage is self-defeating at scale — it resists the wage increases that would force upgrading
External buyer concentration (EU/US) creates geopolitical vulnerability — GSP/EBA preferences can be withdrawn
lessons: Bangladesh demonstrates that sustained growth on a single export pillar is achievable for decades — but the longer it runs, the harder structural transformation becomes. The sector's political power grows with its economic share. For Cambodia, the RMG parallel is direct: EBA partial withdrawal in 2020 was the first stress test of a structure Bangladesh faces amplified.

label: Sri Lanka infrastructure debt trap — fiscal collapse via prestige project overextension
sources: IMF Sri Lanka 2022 Article IV and Emergency Financing
ADB Sri Lanka Country Partnership Strategy 2018
Chatham House Sri Lanka Debt Analysis 2022
context: Post-civil war (2009) Sri Lanka embarked on an ambitious infrastructure programme — highways, airports, ports, and power plants — funded heavily by Chinese and international commercial loans at non-concessional rates. The Hambantota Port became the emblematic case. GDP growth was strong 2010-2014 but masked a deteriorating fiscal position.
shock_or_trigger: The 2019 Easter Sunday bombings hit tourism (25% of GDP) exactly when the fiscal position was already fragile. COVID-19 in 2020 eliminated tourism revenue entirely. The government responded with tax cuts in late 2019 that further eroded the revenue base. By 2021, foreign reserves were near zero. In 2022, Sri Lanka defaulted on its sovereign debt — the first South Asian country to do so since independence.
policy_response: Rajapaksa government doubled down on infrastructure and import substitution (organic farming mandate) rather than IMF engagement. Debt restructuring was delayed by political resistance. When the crisis became acute, the government fell. The new government negotiated an IMF programme — but the terms required deep austerity.

outcomes.export_diversification: No meaningful improvement — tourism and garments remained dominant
outcomes.fiscal_position: Sovereign default April 2022 — first in post-independence history
outcomes.institutional_development: Democratic institutions held but severely stressed

causal_mechanisms: Non-concessional infrastructure debt creates rigid debt service obligations that crowd out social spending in a downturn
Prestige projects (airports, ports) that don't generate sufficient revenue exacerbate fiscal fragility
Revenue concentration in tourism creates catastrophic single-shock vulnerability
Political resistance to IMF engagement delays adjustment and deepens eventual cost
Infrastructure prioritisation over fiscal institution-building leaves no buffer
lessons: The Hambantota model — Chinese-financed infrastructure in exchange for political alignment — is structurally fragile when the host country has a narrow revenue base and high import dependency. The lesson for Cambodia is direct: Funan Techo Canal and similar infrastructure is funded on the assumption of continued growth. If the trade preference base (EBA/GSP) erodes while debt service rises, the fiscal arithmetic can deteriorate faster than the political system can respond.

label: Honduras CAFTA integration — preference-dependent maquila growth without upgrading
sources: ECLAC Honduras Economic Survey 2015
World Bank Honduras Systematic Country Diagnostic 2016
IDB Honduras Trade Integration Report 2018
context: Honduras joined CAFTA-DR in 2005, gaining preferential access to the US market. The maquila (export processing) sector — primarily garments and textiles — expanded rapidly. Honduras positioned itself as a low-cost assembly hub near the US market. GDP growth was moderate but persistent. The maquila sector employed 170,000 workers at peak.
shock_or_trigger: The 2008-2009 global financial crisis hit US import demand sharply. Honduras maquila exports fell 25% in 2009. Recovery was uneven — some capacity relocated to Vietnam and Bangladesh. The structural vulnerability was the lack of any manufacturing capability beyond assembly; the country had not used the preference window to upgrade.
policy_response: Government response was to offer additional incentives to retain maquila — tax holidays, cheaper electricity, wage restraint. No parallel strategy for technology-intensive manufacturing or services. The political economy of maquila (strong employer lobby) blocked labour reforms that might have enabled upgrading.

outcomes.gdp_growth: GDP growth averaged 3.5% 2005-2018 — below regional peers
outcomes.export_diversification: Maquila remained 55% of total exports through 2018
outcomes.fiscal_position: Persistent deficits, high public debt by regional standards
outcomes.institutional_development: Weak — corruption, gang activity, and political instability worsened
outcomes.poverty_reduction: Poverty fell slowly from 65% to 55% — insufficient for income level

causal_mechanisms: CAFTA preferences created growth but not upgrading — the preference margin was too easily competed away
Geographic proximity to a large market is an advantage but also a trap — it locks in assembly rather than capability
Maquila employer lobby captures trade policy, preventing diversification investments
Weak institutions mean FDI flows into enclaves rather than generating local linkages
lessons: Honduras demonstrates the ceiling of pure assembly-sector FDI attraction without complementary upgrading. CAFTA gave Honduras preferential market access — equivalent to Cambodia's EBA/ASEAN position — but the country used it to maximise short-term employment rather than long-term capability. The structural lesson for Cambodia: trade preference access that is not actively used to build domestic capability and institutional depth leaves the economy exposed to competitive displacement when the preference margin narrows.

label: Sovereign Default, Convertibility Collapse, and Heterodox Recovery
sources: IMF Independent Evaluation Office, Lessons from the Crisis in Argentina (2004)
IMF Staff Country Report No. 03/226, Argentina Article IV Consultation (2003)
CIGI Papers No. 110, An Analysis of Argentina’s 2001 Debt Crisis and Restructuring (Octubre 2016)
CRS Report RL32637, Argentina’s Sovereign Debt Restructuring (2005)
CRS Report RL31582, The Argentine Financial Crisis: A Chronology of Events (2003)
NACLA, Argentina 20 Years After La Crisis del 2001 (2021)

context.political_economy: Federal democratic republic; currency peg maintained through austerity; IMF-supported programs from 1994; large primary deficit entering recession

shock_or_trigger.event: After four years of deepening recession, Argentina’s government collapsed in December 2001 and suspended all debt payments — at the time the largest sovereign default in history (USD 100 billion)
key_triggers: 1998–2002 recession: GDP fell 20% cumulatively over three years
Currency peg (convertibility) made export adjustment impossible — peso was 3.5x overvalued in real effective terms by 2001
Debt-to-GDP surged from 47% (1999) to 164% (2002) as GDP collapsed and peso devalued
Corralito (bank freeze) December 2001: government restricted withdrawals to USD 250/week, triggering mass protests
Five presidents in 12 days: De la Rúa resigned December 20, 2001; Rodriguez Saá announced default December 22
IMF austerity program (March 2001) explicitly assumed 2.5% GDP growth for 2001; actual outcome was -3.7%
shock_or_trigger.immediate_external_effect: Total reserves collapsed from USD 27B (1999) to USD 10.5B (2002); sovereign credit rating hit CCC; capital flight, poverty rate surged to 53% by 2002

policy_response.growth_model_invoked: Post-default heterodox recovery: competitive real exchange rate + primary fiscal surplus + commodity export windfall; deliberate rejection of IMF structural conditionality
instruments: January 2002: Convertibility abandoned, peso floated; immediate 3:1 devaluation (eventually 3.9:1 by mid-2002)
Pesification of contracts: dollar-denominated domestic obligations converted to pesos at 1:1, partially protecting debtors
Primary fiscal surplus built from 0.6% GDP (2002) to 5.1% GDP (2004) through tax collection reform and expenditure compression
Competitive exchange rate sustained: real devaluation made Argentine exports globally competitive for the first time since 1991
2005 debt restructuring: 76% of defaulted bonds brought out of default with 65–70% face-value haircut; holdout litigation continued until 2016
January 2006: Kirchner repaid remaining USD 9.81B to IMF in single payment, severing conditionality relationship
what_was_not_done: No independent central bank reform: monetary policy remained subordinate to fiscal needs
Structural reforms to labour market, pension system, and provincial finances deferred
Holdout bondholder litigation left unresolved for 15 years, restricting capital market access
Inflation management tools were weak: inflation re-emerged as chronic problem post-2006

growth.note: V-shaped recovery: sharpest contraction in 2002, then three consecutive years above 8%. Devaluation + commodity boom drove export-led rebound

fiscal.note: Debt-to-GDP fell from 164% in 2002 to 8.3% by 2013 — one of the most dramatic debt-to-GDP reversals in emerging market history, driven by growth and restructuring haircut

social.note: Poverty halved in four years post-default. Conditional cash transfers (Plan Jefes) cushioned impact during adjustment

unintended_consequences: Chronic inflation returned by 2007: the same heterodox policies that drove recovery (fiscal expansion, low rates) eventually embedded inflationary expectations
Capital market isolation: Argentina was shut out of international bond markets from 2002 to 2016 due to holdout litigation
Pesification created massive wealth transfers: dollar depositors received pesos at unfavorable rates, generating lasting legal and social conflict
Recurring cycle: Argentina repeated a comparable crisis in 2018 (Macri IMF program) and 2019–2020, suggesting structural vulnerabilities were not resolved

confirmed: Currency peg overvaluation was the primary structural cause: the convertibility plan made adjustment through exchange rate impossible, forcing all adjustment through deflation and unemployment
Procyclical IMF austerity deepened recession: cutting spending into a recession reduced growth, which increased the debt-to-GDP ratio, which triggered more austerity — a doom loop
Post-default devaluation enabled export competitiveness: the peso’s collapse made Argentine goods globally competitive almost overnight, particularly in soy, manufacturing, and tourism
Commodity supercycle (2003–2011) was exogenous and favorable: Chinese demand for Argentine soy and metals provided fiscal windfall that funded recovery without reform
contested: Whether the IMF or Argentina’s fiscal policy was the primary cause is disputed: IMF self-assessment (2004) acknowledges its program design failed; Argentine government blamed IMF conditionality exclusively
Whether heterodox recovery model was sustainable: post-2007 inflation suggests the model had an expiry date; growth slowed sharply once commodity boom faded

lessons.what_worked: Abandoning an unsustainable peg decisively rather than defending it with austerity; building primary fiscal surplus post-default; using competitive exchange rate to drive export recovery; negotiating a deep debt haircut that restored debt sustainability
lessons.what_failed: IMF procyclical austerity in recession: cutting spending to meet deficit targets during a demand shock turned recession into depression; defending the currency peg to the last depleted reserves and deepened the inevitable adjustment
lessons.structural_failure: The convertibility plan created a decade of false stability by pricing out adjustment mechanisms. When the peg broke, all the suppressed adjustment happened at once, compressing into 2001–2002
lessons.transferability_notes: Directly applicable to any country with USD-denominated debt, a fixed or managed exchange rate, and a single dominant export sector. The sequence — peg + austerity + recession + collapse + devaluation + recovery — is a recurring pattern across emerging markets (Ecuador 1999, Turkey 2001, Greece 2010)
lessons.cambodia_2029_relevance: Cambodia is highly dollarised (USD is de facto currency), carries significant Chinese USD-denominated debt, and is pursuing de-dollarisation (Riel/Bakong) while simultaneously planning EBA-transition fiscal adjustments. If EBA loss triggers a revenue shortfall and Cambodia tightens fiscal policy into a slowdown — the Argentine procyclicality trap is the exact risk. The NBC’s de-dollarisation timeline (Riel to 30% of transactions by 2028) and MEF’s USD-denominated budget assumptions may not be simultaneously achievable — this is Cambodia’s version of the convertibility trap.

label: Asian Financial Crisis: Patronage System Collapse and Contested IMF Recovery
sources: IMF, Recovery from the Asian Crisis and the Role of the IMF (Issues Brief, June 2000)
IMF Occasional Paper 178, IMF-Supported Programs in Indonesia, Korea, and Thailand: A Preliminary Assessment (1999)
ADB, Financial Crisis and Recovery: Patterns of Adjustment in East Asia, 1996–99 (2002)
IMF, Realizing Indonesia’s Economic Potential: Chapter 2, Twenty Years after the Asian Financial Crisis (2018)
Federal Reserve History, Asian Financial Crisis (2013)
Indonesia-Investments.com, Asian Financial Crisis — Indonesia (2014)

context.short_term_private_debt_pct_of_reserves: High — unhedged USD-denominated private loans were the primary vulnerability
context.political_economy: Suharto’s New Order regime (1966–1998): single-party authoritarian state, crony capitalist networks, patronage-based licensing system; IMF conditionality explicitly targeted cronies’ monopolies

shock_or_trigger.event: Thai baht collapse July 1997 spread to Indonesian rupiah by August 1997; rupiah floated and immediately began depreciating; by January 1998 the rupiah had lost 70% of its value within days
key_triggers: Large unhedged private USD-denominated short-term debt — when rupiah fell, corporate debt-to-GDP exploded
IMF closed 16 insolvent banks in November 1997, triggering bank runs on remaining institutions
Suharto health speculation and political uncertainty compounded investor flight
El Niño drought (1997–98): forest fires and poor harvests added food crisis to financial crisis
Second IMF agreement (January 1998) included 50-point reform program targeting Suharto’s patronage network — Suharto refused to implement it faithfully
Suharto resigned May 1998 after mass protests; political transition added governance uncertainty
shock_or_trigger.immediate_external_effect: GDP fell 13.1% in 1998 — deepest contraction of any Asian crisis country; poverty rate spiked; rupiah at low of 16,000 per USD (versus 2,400 pre-crisis)

policy_response.growth_model_invoked: IMF-led stabilisation: fiscal contraction, high interest rates, bank closures, structural reform; then gradual relaxation as stabilisation took hold in mid-1998; post-Suharto: banking sector restructuring and democratic transition
instruments: Three IMF stand-by arrangements: November 1997 (USD 10B), augmented July 1998 (USD 1.4B); total international support USD 43B including World Bank and bilateral donors
Indonesian Bank Restructuring Agency (IBRA) established January 1998 to absorb bad bank assets
Blanket government guarantee on all bank liabilities to restore confidence
High interest rates (to 65%+) to defend currency — later shown to be counterproductive as it increased corporate NPLs
Third IMF agreement (April 1998): more flexible, allowed food subsidies and wider deficit, added bankruptcy courts
Post-Suharto: democratic transition (1998–2004) restructured political economy away from crony networks
what_was_not_done: First IMF program did not address Suharto patronage system — the structural cause of financial fragility; second program tried and was refused
High interest rates were maintained too long: deepened corporate distress rather than defending currency
Bank closures without adequate deposit insurance caused bank runs rather than restoring confidence

growth.note: Recovery was slower than Korea and Thailand — Indonesia’s deeper political transition delayed economic normalisation. Full pre-crisis growth rates not restored until mid-2000s

banking.note: Decade of banking sector reform transformed Indonesia’s financial system: NPLs fell from crisis levels to below 4% by 2008, enabling Indonesia to weather 2008 global crisis with minimal impact

political.outcome: Suharto resigned May 1998 after 32 years; democratic transition 1998–2004 produced competitive elections, regional autonomy, civil liberties; New Order patronage network dismantled unevenly
political.note: Political transformation was the most consequential long-run outcome — Indonesia became one of Asia’s largest democracies

unintended_consequences: IMF bank closures (November 1997) caused bank runs rather than restoring confidence — the opposite of intended effect
High interest rate prescription increased NPLs and worsened corporate debt crisis rather than defending currency
Blanket bank guarantee created moral hazard: bank owners had reduced incentive to manage risk
Indonesia paid back IMF loans by 2003 and exited IMF relationship early, gaining policy autonomy but at cost of Paris Club debt rescheduling access

confirmed: Unhedged private USD debt was the transmission mechanism: Thai baht collapse caused investors to reassess regional risk; rupiah selling triggered corporate balance sheet deterioration

Patronage system was the structural vulnerability: Suharto’s crony network created moral hazard in banking (politically connected borrowers did not face credit discipline) and prevented IMF reforms from taking hold
Democratic transition enabled structural reform: only after Suharto fell could banking sector restructuring and anti-monopoly measures be credibly implemented
ADB cross-country study finding: recovery speed was driven by export orientation and macroeconomic flexibility, not IMF program design — IMF financial assistance had no independent impact on recovery
contested: Whether IMF prescriptions (high interest rates, bank closures) made the crisis worse is debated: IMF self-assessment acknowledges bank closure design was flawed; standard IMF defence is that without intervention the crisis would have been deeper

Whether political transition was a prerequisite for recovery or a complicating factor: accelerated democratic change created short-term governance uncertainty while enabling long-run institutional improvement

lessons.what_worked: Banking sector restructuring post-crisis: decade of NPL reduction and capital adequacy improvement made Indonesia resilient to 2008 global crisis; democratic transition dismantled patronage networks that made financial system fragile
lessons.what_failed: IMF initial program design: high interest rates, rapid bank closures without deposit insurance, and fiscal contraction into a demand collapse deepened the crisis before stabilisation; conditionality that targeted political patronage without political preconditions for reform was unimplementable
lessons.structural_failure: Patronage-based financial system: banks that lend on political connections rather than creditworthiness accumulate fragility that is invisible until a shock arrives, then collapses catastrophically
lessons.transferability_notes: Highly relevant to any country combining: (1) a patronage-based governance system where elite networks control credit allocation, (2) significant unhedged USD-denominated private debt, (3) a fixed or managed exchange rate. The Indonesia case is also a cautionary model for how IMF conditionality targeting political structures fails when the political authority controls implementation
lessons.cambodia_2029_relevance: Cambodia’s banking system shows early warning signs of Indonesia-1997 structural vulnerabilities: CPP-connected networks dominate credit allocation; household debt has grown rapidly (flagged by analysts in 2024); Chinese USD-denominated project financing creates balance sheet exposure. If a Cambodia-specific shock (EBA loss + Chinese FDI pullback) triggers a credit deterioration, the patronage-banking nexus is the amplification mechanism. The IMF lesson is explicit: conditionality that requires dismantling political patronage networks cannot be externally imposed — it requires internal political change.

label: Rana Plaza: Industrial Disaster as Regulatory Shock and Compliance Transformation
sources: World Bank / IZA Discussion Paper 13782, The Effects of International Scrutiny on Manufacturing Workers: Evidence from the Rana Plaza Collapse in Bangladesh (2020)
ILO, After Rana Plaza: How Has Safety Improved for Garment Workers? (Podcast, April 2023)
Human Rights Watch, Whoever Raises their Head Suffers the Most: Workers’ Rights in Bangladesh’s Garment Factories (2015)
Al Jazeera, Ten Years of Rana Plaza: How Safe is Bangladesh Garment Industry? (April 2023)
ADB, In Bangladesh, ADB Helped Build A Safer and Healthier Workplace (2023)
McKinsey & Company, Bangladesh’s Garment Sector as Frontrunner in Transparency (2021)
QIMA Global Supply Chain Compliance Report (2021)

context.regulatory_baseline: Labour Law Act 2006 existed but was unenforced: Dhaka district had 18 inspectors for 100,000 factories; fines minimal; advance notice given to factory owners before inspections; 37% of female garment workers reported abuse in Q1 2013
context.political_economy: Competitive authoritarian state; garment factory owners held significant political power within Parliament; BGMEA (industry association) had direct government access

events.event: Tazreen Fashions fire, November 2012: 117 workers killed; evidence of locked exit doors
events.significance: International buyers began reassessing Bangladesh sourcing risk

events.event: Rana Plaza collapse, April 24, 2013: 1,134 workers killed, 2,500 injured in nine-storey building collapse; workers had reported cracks in the building the day before and were ordered to return anyway
events.significance: Deadliest industrial disaster in garment industry history; global media coverage triggered immediate buyer and government responses

shock_or_trigger.immediate_external_effect: US suspended GSP trade benefits for Bangladesh in June 2013; EU threatened EBA removal; global brands faced consumer boycotts; insurance and liability questions forced supply chain accountability

policy_response.growth_model_invoked: Compliance-as-competitive-advantage: use safety reform as a tool to retain international buyer confidence and defend export market position, rather than accepting factory relocations to competitors
instruments: Accord on Fire and Building Safety in Bangladesh (May 2013): legally binding agreement between 200+ global brands and Bangladeshi unions; independent inspections, mandatory remediation, worker complaint mechanisms
Alliance for Bangladesh Worker Safety (July 2013): parallel US-retailer-led voluntary initiative covering 700+ factories
Labour Law Amendment (July 2013): eased union registration requirements; added safety provisions
Minimum wage increase: from BDT 3,000 to BDT 5,300/month in 2013 (77% increase); to BDT 8,000 in 2018
Department of Inspection for Factories and Establishments (DIFE) expanded: inspectors increased, digital factory registry created
Rana Plaza Arrangement: compensation fund for survivors (USD 30M raised from international brands)
what_was_not_done: Independent judiciary for labour disputes: factory owners retained political influence over enforcement; prosecution of Rana Plaza owner took years
Freedom of association: union formation easier on paper but employer retaliation increased as unions organized; HRW documented physical abuse of union leaders
Subcontractor oversight: Accord and Alliance covered first-tier factories; informal subcontractors (40%+ of production) remained outside compliance systems

working_conditions.note: Working conditions improved by 0.80 standard deviations in Accord/Alliance-covered districts vs control group — one of the largest measured improvements in manufacturing labour conditions in any peer-reviewed study

wages.note: Contrary to compensating differentials theory (which predicts wages fall when conditions improve), wages increased 10% alongside working condition improvements — international buyer accountability created wage floor

factory_safety.source: Bangladesh Garment Manufacturers and Exporters Association (BGMEA)
factory_safety.note: Over 80% of Bangladesh’s 3,200 RMG factories now internationally compliant by 2023; McKinsey (2021) ranked Bangladesh RMG sector first globally in supply chain transparency

trade_performance.note: Garment exports grew 79% from 2015 to 2022 despite (and partly because of) compliance investment; Bangladesh maintained position as world’s second-largest apparel exporter

unintended_consequences: Rana Plaza survivors: 63%+ did not return to garment work post-disaster (ActionAid survey, 2023) — trauma-driven labour market exit
Compliance cost increased barriers to entry: smaller factories unable to afford remediation closed or consolidated; sector became more concentrated
Accord ended 2018: replacement body (RSC) had weaker legal bindingness than original Accord; brand commitments became voluntary again
Union repression continued: while union registration was easier, employer retaliation against organizers increased — legal reform did not change power dynamics

confirmed: Legal bindingness of Accord was the critical differentiator: the Accord’s contractual obligations (brands could be sued for non-compliance) outperformed the Alliance’s voluntary commitments in achieving remediation
International buyer reputational risk created private enforcement: brands faced consumer boycotts and shareholder pressure; this created stronger enforcement incentives than government regulation

Wage increase alongside condition improvement shows supply chain leverage works: when brands accepted accountability for supplier conditions, worker outcomes improved without the trade-off standard economics predicts
Accident rate in covered factories dropped dramatically: Accord-covered factories showed measurable structural remediation; worker fatalities from preventable causes declined
contested: Whether reform was sustainable: when Accord’s legal bindingness ended in 2018, voluntary successor showed weaker compliance discipline; long-run sustainability of buyer-driven standards without state enforcement is disputed
Distribution of gains: formal-sector workers in covered factories gained; informal subcontract workers and Rana Plaza survivors who left the sector did not

lessons.what_worked: Legally binding buyer agreements with independent verification and worker complaint mechanisms; minimum wage increase as floor-setting mechanism; public international pressure creating private enforcement that outperformed government regulation
lessons.what_failed: Government’s own enforcement capacity was not rebuilt: DIFE expansion was insufficient; when international buyer accountability receded, domestic enforcement did not step in; union protection on paper but not in practice
lessons.critical_gap: Subcontractor coverage: compliance systems covered visible first-tier factories but informal production networks (where worst conditions persist) remained outside accountability frameworks
lessons.transferability_notes: Directly applicable to any export-dependent garment economy facing international regulatory pressure. The key variable is buyer leverage: the Accord model works when brands have reputational exposure large enough to create private enforcement. Countries producing for less brand-conscious markets (commodity garments, regional buyers) cannot rely on buyer-driven accountability
lessons.cambodia_2029_relevance: Cambodia’s garment sector faces a comparable inflection: EBA graduation removes the EU’s preferential access, but EU buyers often require ILO compliance standards as a condition of continued sourcing. If EBA loss is accompanied by buyer reputational pressure on labour standards — the Rana Plaza pattern applies. Cambodia’s risk is the opposite of Bangladesh’s success scenario: if labour conditions do not improve alongside market access loss, buyers may shift to Bangladesh or Vietnam rather than stay. The GMAC (Garment Manufacturers Association in Cambodia) model of government-industry co-regulation needs a legal bindingness upgrade that it currently lacks.

label: NAFTA Maquiladora Surge: Market Access Without Labour Upgrading
sources: Carnegie Endowment for International Peace, Mexican Employment, Productivity and Income a Decade after NAFTA (2004)
Economic Policy Institute, NAFTA at Seven: Its Impact on Workers in All Three Nations (2001)
US International Trade Commission, The Impact of Trade Agreements on US Workers (2003)
Comité Fronterizo de Obreras, 23 Years of NAFTA: A View from Inside the Maquiladoras (2017)
Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas, NAFTA and Maquiladoras: Is the Growth Connected? (2001)
Florida State University Journal of Transnational Law & Policy, Workers’ Rights in the Mexican Maquiladora Sector (2005)

context.regulatory_baseline: Maquiladora Decree (1989) already established export processing framework; NAFTA created free trade zone with US and Canada from January 1, 1994; peso devaluation crisis hit simultaneously December 1994
context.political_economy: PRI one-party state transitioning to multiparty democracy; labour unions corporatist and controlled; NAFTA sold domestically as modernisation and development pathway

shock_or_trigger.event: NAFTA entered into force January 1, 1994, eliminating tariffs across US-Mexico-Canada and massively expanding maquiladora eligibility beyond border zones; simultaneously, peso devaluation crisis (December 1994–January 1995) made Mexican labour dramatically cheaper in dollar terms
key_commitments: Elimination of most tariffs between US, Mexico, Canada over 15 years
NAFTA Side Agreement on Labor (NAALC): created complaints mechanism but no binding labour standards or sanctions
Maquiladora expanded from border zones to all of Mexico
Investor-state dispute mechanism (Chapter 11) gave foreign investors right to sue government over regulation
shock_or_trigger.immediate_external_effect: US-Mexico trade grew from USD 81B (1993) to USD 345B (2006); maquiladora employment surged from 420,000 (1993) to 1.3M (2000); peso devaluation of 50%+ made Mexican labour 50% cheaper in dollar terms overnight

policy_response.growth_model_invoked: Export-led growth through maquiladora FDI attraction; government positioned NAFTA as the modernisation mechanism that would raise living standards through integration into North American production networks
instruments: IMMEX program (2006): merged Maquiladora Decree with PITEX, expanding eligibility and flexibility for exporters
No independent labour standard enforcement: NAALC side agreement had no sanctions; labour complaints were processed through political rather than judicial mechanisms
Duty-free import of inputs and machinery for maquiladoras: zero-tariff inputs for re-export production
Special Economic Zones: northern border states became integrated into US production networks for auto, electronics, apparel
what_was_not_done: No binding labour standards: NAFTA’s labour side agreement had no sanctions; wage convergence with US was not a NAFTA objective and did not occur
No domestic supply chain development: maquiladoras operated as enclaves — inputs imported, output exported, minimal linkage to Mexican economy
No agricultural transition support: NAFTA eliminated corn tariffs, displacing 1–2M small farmers who then either migrated to maquiladora cities or to the US
No wage floor mechanism: real wages in manufacturing fell in the decade after NAFTA despite productivity gains

employment.note: Employment boom peaked in 2000 at 1.3M workers, then shed 250,000 jobs by 2003 as China entered WTO and undercut Mexican labour costs; net gain of 550,000 maquiladora jobs over pre-NAFTA baseline

trade.note: Trade quadrupled in 13 years; Mexico became US’s second-largest trading partner

wages.real_manufacturing_wage_trend: Fell in the decade post-NAFTA despite productivity growth
wages.note: Carnegie Endowment study (2004): real wages in manufacturing were lower in 2003 than in 1994 except in microenterprises. Maquiladora workers’ wages rose in nominal terms but were consistently below 10% of US manufacturing wages — wage convergence did not occur

unintended_consequences: China WTO accession (2001) exposed the model’s fragility: Mexico lost an estimated 500,000 maquiladora jobs to China by 2004 because the model was built on low wages, not capabilities
Agricultural displacement: NAFTA corn tariff elimination displaced 1–2M Mexican farmers; migration to border cities and US surged — annual migration doubled from 370,000 (1993) to 770,000 (2000)
Enclave economy: maquiladoras remained isolated from Mexican domestic economy; local content rarely exceeded 3–5%; value added in Mexico was primarily labour cost
Wage suppression: corporatist unions signed agreements without worker input; real wages did not converge with productivity growth; poverty reduction was slower than trade growth implied

confirmed: Market access alone does not produce development: NAFTA provided duty-free access and FDI flows, but without domestic supply chain development, value capture remained minimal — Mexican economy gained jobs but not capabilities
Labour standards vacuum enabled a race to the bottom: NAALC’s absence of sanctions meant employers could violate labour law without consequence; corporatist unions suppressed worker organisation

China competition exposed the low-wage model’s limits: once China entered WTO (2001) at comparable wage levels with superior manufacturing scale, Mexico’s cost advantage disappeared in electronics and apparel

Currency devaluation (1994–95) amplified NAFTA effects: the peso crisis made Mexican labour dramatically cheaper in dollar terms and drove the maquiladora surge more than NAFTA itself in the short run
contested: Whether NAFTA was net positive or negative for Mexican workers is disputed: macro trade growth was real; micro wage and employment data for manufacturing workers is more pessimistic; agricultural workers were clear net losers

Counterfactual: what would have happened without NAFTA is unknown; proponents argue Mexico would have had fewer manufacturing jobs; critics argue domestic-market industrialisation would have been more welfare-enhancing

lessons.what_worked: Trade agreement as FDI magnet: NAFTA’s investment protections and tariff elimination did attract substantial FDI into manufacturing; auto sector in particular developed genuine capabilities over time
lessons.what_failed: Market access without labour standards produced wage suppression rather than development; maquiladora enclave structure prevented economic linkages that would have raised domestic capability; agricultural sector received no transition support
lessons.critical_gap: No domestic supply chain requirement: maquiladoras imported nearly all inputs and exported nearly all output — Mexican economy got jobs but not technology transfer, skills upgrading, or supply chain integration. Honduras post-CAFTA repeated this exact pattern.
lessons.transferability_notes: The Mexico-NAFTA case is the template for what happens when trade market access is secured without accompanying (1) binding labour standards, (2) domestic supply chain development policy, and (3) transition support for displaced workers. It is the cautionary complement to Mauritius’s success — same preferential access instrument, opposite development outcome
lessons.cambodia_2029_relevance: Cambodia’s garment sector is structurally analogous to Mexico’s maquiladora model: export-oriented, foreign-owned, low domestic value capture, imported inputs (Chinese fabrics), labour-cost competitive. The Mexico lesson is that this model has an expiry date — it lasts only as long as Cambodia is the cheapest option. When Vietnam or Myanmar undercuts Cambodia’s labour costs, Cambodia faces the same China-shock that Mexico faced in 2001. The 2029 transition window is Cambodia’s equivalent of Mexico’s need to move up the value chain before cost competition eliminated the maquiladora advantage — and Cambodia, like Mexico, has no domestic supply chain policy that would enable the transition.
